intro:
The seven churches of Asia are the congregations addressed by the risen Christ in Revelation 2–3, each receiving a letter dictated to John on Patmos (Revelation 1:11). They were real first-century churches set on a Roman postal road through the province of Asia (western Asia Minor), yet the number seven — completeness — has long invited the reading that together they portray the whole church in every age. Each letter follows a common pattern: a title of Christ drawn from the opening vision, a commendation and/or rebuke, a call to "hear what the Spirit says," and a promise to the one who conquers.

The seven churches

They are addressed in geographical order, the route a messenger would travel north from Ephesus and then inland:

Ephesus — the leading city of Asia; praised for endurance and discernment but charged with forsaking its first love (Revelation 2:1–7).

Smyrna — a suffering, impoverished congregation that receives no rebuke, only a call to faithfulness unto death and the crown of life (Revelation 2:8–11).

Pergamum — held fast Christ’s name "where Satan’s throne is," yet tolerated the teaching of Balaam and the Nicolaitans (Revelation 2:12–17).

Thyatira — commended for love, faith, and service that increased, but rebuked for tolerating the prophetess "Jezebel" (Revelation 2:18–29).

Sardis — had a reputation for life but was spiritually dead; called to "wake up" and strengthen what remained (Revelation 3:1–6).

Philadelphia — of little strength yet faithful; before it Christ sets an open door no one can shut (Revelation 3:7–13).

Laodicea — lukewarm and self-satisfied, counseled to buy gold refined in fire while Christ stands at the door and knocks (Revelation 3:14–22).

link_tables.title: The Seven Churches

cells: Commended for toil and doctrine, yet had abandoned its first love
Poor yet rich; told to be faithful unto death
Dwelt where Satan's throne is; tolerated false teaching
Growing in love and service, but tolerated 'Jezebel'
Had a name for being alive but was dead; told to wake up
Little strength, yet kept Christ's word; an open door set before it
Rich in its own eyes but wretched; Christ stands at the door and knocks

link_tables.note: Addressed in Revelation 2–3. Each letter ends with a promise to "the one who conquers." Click a city to read more.
